project.name: barstock
project.description: Single-counter liquor shop inventory & billing system for a shop in Odisha, India — FastAPI/SQLAlchemy/Postgres backend plus a React/TypeScript/Vite/Tailwind SPA frontend.

nodes.name: env.py
nodes.summary: Alembic migration environment configuration that wires the async SQLAlchemy engine and app models into offline and online migration runs.
nodes.languageNotes: Uses an async SQLAlchemy engine bridged into Alembic's sync migration runner via run_sync.

nodes.name: run_migrations_offline
nodes.summary: Configures and runs Alembic migrations in offline mode using a database URL string without a live connection.

nodes.name: run_async_migrations
nodes.summary: Creates an async engine from Alembic config and runs migrations synchronously within an async connection context.

nodes.name: run_migrations_online
nodes.summary: Entry point that drives asyncio to execute the async online migration flow.

nodes.name: LotPublic

nodes.name: low_stock.py
nodes.summary: Service computing which products are at or below their low-stock threshold by deriving current stock from received lot quantities minus sold invoice quantities.
nodes.languageNotes: Uses SQLAlchemy subqueries with union_all and coalesce to derive net stock (received minus sold) in a single database round trip.

nodes.name: _stock_per_product
nodes.summary: Computes net current stock per product for a shop by combining received-lot-quantity and sold-invoice-quantity subqueries.

nodes.name: compute_low_stock
nodes.summary: Builds a sorted list of products whose current stock is at or below their effective low-stock threshold (per-product override or shop default).

nodes.name: LowStockRow
nodes.summary: Data container pairing a product with its current stock and effective low-stock threshold.

nodes.name: test_checkout.py
nodes.summary: Integration test suite covering checkout finalization: single/multi-line invoices, split payments, idempotency, concurrency on last-unit stock, role permissions, invoice immutability, audit logging, and PDF generation.

nodes.name: test_two_concurrent_finalizes_for_last_unit_only_one_wins
nodes.summary: Verifies that when two concurrent checkout requests race for the last unit of stock, only one finalizes successfully and the other fails cleanly.

nodes.name: test_idempotency_key_replay_returns_same_invoice
nodes.summary: Verifies that replaying a checkout request with the same idempotency key returns the original invoice instead of creating a duplicate.

nodes.name: test_finalized_invoice_lines_are_immutable
nodes.summary: Verifies that invoice lines cannot be modified once an invoice has been finalized.

nodes.name: test_finalize_writes_an_invoicing_log
nodes.summary: Verifies that finalizing a checkout writes an InvoicingLog audit record.

nodes.name: test_invoice_pdf_is_a_real_pdf
nodes.summary: Verifies that the invoice PDF endpoint returns a valid PDF document for a finalized invoice.

nodes.name: test_lots.py
nodes.summary: Integration test suite for lot (stock-receiving) endpoints, covering creation, role permissions, barcode validation, shop scoping, audit logging, and derived stock increases.

nodes.name: test_receiving_increases_derivable_stock
nodes.summary: Verifies that receiving a lot increases the product's derivable current stock as computed by the low-stock service.

nodes.name: test_receiving_writes_a_stockin_log
nodes.summary: Verifies that creating a lot writes a corresponding StockinLog audit record.

nodes.name: test_lot_listing_is_shop_scoped
nodes.summary: Verifies that listing lots only returns lots belonging to the actor's own shop.

nodes.name: test_duplicate_barcode_in_lot_rejected
nodes.summary: Verifies that a lot creation request containing duplicate barcodes across its lines is rejected.

nodes.name: test_voids.py
nodes.summary: Integration test suite for invoice voiding, covering direct pre-EOD voids with stock restoration, void-request/approve/reject workflow for signed-off invoices, and audit logging of void actions.

nodes.name: test_pre_eod_void_restores_stock
nodes.summary: Verifies that directly voiding an invoice before end-of-day sign-off restores the sold quantities back to stock.

nodes.name: test_owner_approving_pending_void_creates_reversal
nodes.summary: Verifies that an owner approving a pending void request creates a reversing transaction for a signed-off invoice.

nodes.name: test_void_actions_write_invoicing_logs
nodes.summary: Verifies that void-related actions (request, approve, reject, direct void) each write an InvoicingLog audit record.

nodes.name: checkout.py
nodes.summary: Pydantic request/response schemas for the checkout API, including cart line validation, payment total validation, and invoice serialization.

nodes.name: CheckoutLine
nodes.summary: Input schema for a single cart line consisting of a product barcode and quantity.

nodes.name: PaymentInput
nodes.summary: Input schema for a single payment consisting of a payment mode and amount.

nodes.name: CheckoutFinalizeRequest
nodes.summary: Request schema for finalizing a checkout, bundling cart lines and payments with a validator ensuring payments sum to a non-empty amount.

nodes.name: InvoiceLinePublic
nodes.summary: Response schema serializing an invoice line's product, quantity, unit price, and line total.

nodes.name: PaymentPublic
nodes.summary: Response schema serializing a payment's mode and amount.

nodes.name: InvoicePublic
nodes.summary: Response schema serializing a full invoice with its lines, payments, status, and sign-off state for API consumers.

nodes.name: CheckoutFinalizeResponse
nodes.summary: Response wrapper indicating the finalized invoice and whether the response was returned from an idempotent replay.

nodes.name: checkout.py
nodes.summary: Core checkout service that validates cart lines against live stock, enforces idempotency and EOD sign-off rules, and finalizes an invoice with payments inside a single transaction.
nodes.languageNotes: Uses row-level locking (with_for_update) and correlated subqueries to compute live stock (received minus sold) before allowing checkout, protecting against overselling under concurrency.

nodes.name: CheckoutError
nodes.summary: Domain exception raised for any checkout validation failure (bad idempotency key, insufficient stock, EOD already signed off, payment mismatch).

nodes.name: CartLine
nodes.summary: Simple data holder representing a requested cart line (barcode and quantity) passed into the checkout service.

nodes.name: PaymentLine
nodes.summary: Data holder representing a single payment tender (mode and amount) submitted during checkout.

nodes.name: FinalizeResult
nodes.summary: Return wrapper from finalize_checkout carrying the resulting invoice and a flag indicating whether the call was an idempotent replay.

nodes.name: _is_today_signed_off
nodes.summary: Checks whether the shop's business day has already been signed off for end-of-day, blocking further checkouts if so.

nodes.name: _resolve_products_for_cart
nodes.summary: Resolves cart barcodes to active Product records for the shop, raising CheckoutError for any barcode that cannot be matched.

nodes.name: _current_stock_for
nodes.summary: Computes live stock per product as received lot quantity minus finalized invoice quantity, using unioned aggregate subqueries so partial-only data (received-only or sold-only) is still counted.
nodes.languageNotes: Combines two GROUP BY subqueries with an outer join plus a UNION ALL branch to cover products present in only one side of the join.

nodes.name: finalize_checkout
nodes.summary: End-to-end checkout finalization: validates idempotency key, cart totals, and EOD status, locks affected products and the shop row, verifies sufficient stock, then persists the Invoice, InvoiceLines, Payments, and an idempotency record in one transaction.
nodes.languageNotes: Locks Product and Shop rows with with_for_update() to serialize concurrent checkouts against the same stock and invoice numbering sequence.

nodes.name: invoice_pdf.py
nodes.summary: Renders a finalized invoice as a printable PDF receipt (shop header, line items, payment breakdown) using ReportLab.
nodes.languageNotes: Builds the document as a ReportLab 'story' of flowables (Paragraph, Table, Spacer) rendered into an in-memory BytesIO buffer.

nodes.name: render_invoice_pdf
nodes.summary: Builds and returns the raw PDF bytes for a given invoice, including shop details, GSTIN/excise info, itemized lines, and payment methods.

nodes.name: voids.py
nodes.summary: Service handling invoice voiding: immediate same-day voids, and a request/approve/reject workflow for post-EOD voids that require owner sign-off and create reversal entries.
nodes.languageNotes: Post-EOD voids are modeled as a two-step workflow (request then owner approval) since the original invoice cannot simply be deleted after end-of-day sign-off; approval creates a reversal invoice/lines rather than mutating the original.

nodes.name: VoidError
nodes.summary: Domain exception raised for invalid void operations (invoice not found, already voided, wrong status for the requested void path).

nodes.name: VoidResult
nodes.summary: Return wrapper from approve_post_eod_void carrying the original invoice and the generated reversal invoice.

nodes.name: _load_invoice_for_void
nodes.summary: Loads an invoice scoped to a shop by id, raising VoidError if it does not exist for that shop.

nodes.name: direct_void
nodes.summary: Immediately voids an invoice on the same business day (before EOD sign-off), marking it voided with the actor, reason, and timestamp.

nodes.name: request_post_eod_void
nodes.summary: Records a pending void request for an invoice that falls after EOD sign-off, to be later approved or rejected by an owner.

nodes.name: approve_post_eod_void
nodes.summary: Owner-approves a pending post-EOD void: locks the shop row, creates a reversal Invoice with negated InvoiceLines and Payments, and marks the original invoice voided.

nodes.name: reject_post_eod_void
nodes.summary: Owner-rejects a pending post-EOD void request, leaving the original invoice unchanged and closing the request.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: The README introduces Barstock: a single-counter liquor shop inventory and billing system built for a real shop in Odisha, India. It lays out the tech stack (FastAPI, SQLAlchemy 2.0 async, Postgres, Alembic, structlog, JWT auth) and the app/ directory layout that mirrors the layers you'll walk through in this tour: models, schemas, api, security, and services.

tour.title: Application Entry Point
tour.description: app/main.py is where the FastAPI app is assembled. The create_app() factory builds the app, the lifespan context manager starts the DB engine and a background low-stock polling task on startup, and every API router (auth, checkout, voids, lots, products, shops, staff, dashboard, users, health) gets mounted here. This file is the highest fan-out node in the codebase — it is the map of everything the system exposes.
tour.languageLesson: FastAPI's app-factory pattern (create_app()) and lifespan context managers let you control startup/shutdown behavior (opening a DB engine, spawning background asyncio tasks) in one place instead of scattering globals.

tour.title: Configuration, Database & Logging Bootstrap
tour.description: Before any request-handling code runs, three foundational modules wire the runtime together. config.py uses pydantic-settings to load JWT secrets, the Postgres DSN, and CORS/job-interval settings from environment variables behind a cached get_settings(). db.py builds the async SQLAlchemy engine, session factory, and declarative Base that every model inherits from. logging_config.py sets up structlog so every layer emits structured JSON logs via a shared get_logger() helper.
tour.languageLesson: pydantic-settings validates environment variables at startup (failing fast on misconfiguration) rather than raising cryptic errors deep inside request handlers. SQLAlchemy 2.0's async engine/session pattern requires explicit session-per-request scoping, which is why db.py exposes a session factory rather than a single global session.

tour.title: Security & Request Dependencies
tour.description: Authentication rests on two small, focused modules: jwt.py issues and decodes JWT access tokens, and passwords.py hashes/verifies credentials with bcrypt. app/api/deps.py ties them into FastAPI's dependency-injection system, providing db_session, get_current_user (decoding the bearer token), require_role (RBAC guard), and resolve_write_shop_id/require_shop_scope (multi-tenant write scoping). Nearly every API router in this project depends on deps.py, making it one of the highest fan-in files in the codebase.
tour.languageLesson: FastAPI dependencies (functions passed to Depends()) let you compose cross-cutting concerns — auth, DB sessions, role checks — as reusable, testable functions instead of decorators or middleware, and each endpoint declares exactly which dependencies it needs.

tour.title: Core Domain: Users & Shops
tour.description: The shop is the tenant boundary in this multi-shop system: models/shop.py defines the Shop entity holding shop code, GSTIN, excise duty rate, invoice numbering state, and low-stock defaults. models/user.py defines the User account and the UserRole enum (superadmin/owner/receiver/cashier) with a relationship back to Shop. Together these two files are the most depended-upon models in the codebase, underpinning every role check and shop-scoped query you'll see in later API and service layers.

tour.title: Inventory & Audit Log Models
tour.description: Building on the Shop/User foundation, product.py defines a Product (a specific brand/bottle-size SKU) with barcode, price, and low-stock threshold. lot.py models stock-receiving lots and their per-product line items — this is how new stock enters the system. log.py defines a shared _BaseLog table with three subtype tables (InvoicingLog, StockinLog, AdminLog) that inherit from it, giving the shop an audit trail of every invoicing, stock-in, and admin action.
tour.languageLesson: SQLAlchemy 2.0's declarative mapping supports single-table inheritance (_BaseLog subclassed by InvoicingLog/StockinLog/AdminLog), letting shared audit-log columns live in one place while each subtype adds its own fields.

tour.title: The Transactional Core: Invoices
tour.description: models/invoice.py is the heart of the billing domain: it defines Invoice, InvoiceLine, Payment, EOD sign-off records, and idempotency keys in one file. This is the second-highest fan-in file in the project — nearly every service and test that touches money or stock ultimately reads or writes through these tables. Notice how idempotency keys and EOD sign-off state are modeled directly alongside the invoice, not bolted on separately, reflecting decisions made early in the project's design (see the harness docs referenced in the README).

tour.title: Service Layer: Checkout & End-of-Day
tour.description: services/checkout.py is the core transaction: it validates cart lines against live stock, enforces idempotency and EOD sign-off rules, and finalizes an invoice with payments — all inside a single database transaction, including protecting the last unit of stock under concurrent requests. services/eod.py implements end-of-day sign-off, computing day totals with payment-mode breakdowns and retrieving sign-off history and the pending void queue. These services keep business rules independent of HTTP concerns, so the same logic is reused by both the API layer and the test suite.
tour.languageLesson: Keeping business logic in a service layer (rather than inline in FastAPI route handlers) makes transaction boundaries explicit and lets you unit-test checkout/EOD rules — including concurrency edge cases like double-selling the last unit — without spinning up HTTP requests.

tour.title: Service Layer: Voids, Low-Stock & Receipts
tour.description: services/voids.py handles invoice voiding: immediate same-day voids, plus a request/approve/reject workflow for post-EOD voids that require owner sign-off and generate reversal entries. services/low_stock.py computes which products are at or below threshold by deriving current stock as received lot quantities minus sold invoice quantities — notice there's no separate 'stock' column; stock is always derived via SQL aggregation over lots and invoices. services/invoice_pdf.py renders a finalized invoice as a printable PDF receipt using ReportLab, pulling in the shop's own tax settings rather than hardcoding them.

tour.title: API Layer: Identity & Shop Management
tour.description: auth.py exposes superadmin and shop-user login, verifying credentials and issuing JWTs. users.py returns the current user's public profile. staff.py lets shop owners create and list staff/receiver/cashier accounts scoped to their own shop. shops.py lets superadmins list all shops and lets owners view/update their own shop's GSTIN, excise rate, and low-stock defaults. All four routers lean on the deps.py guards from Step 4 to enforce who can do what.

tour.title: API Layer: Inventory
tour.description: lots.py exposes endpoints to create, list, and fetch stock-receiving lots, writing stock-in logs and enforcing shop scoping as each lot arrives. products.py handles product management: create, list, barcode lookup, update, and bulk CSV import, all with unique-barcode validation. These two routers are the entry points for the inventory models from Step 6 — every lot created here increases the derived stock computed by low_stock.py in Step 9.

tour.title: API Layer: Checkout, Voids & Dashboard
tour.description: checkout.py handles checkout finalization and invoice retrieval (including PDF export), delegating straight to the checkout service from Step 8 and translating domain errors into HTTP responses. voids.py exposes the immediate and request/approve/reject void workflows from services/voids.py. dashboard.py ties it all together for shop owners: EOD sign-off, day totals, sign-off history, the pending void queue, and low-stock reporting — the single screen an owner checks at the end of the day.

tour.title: Database Schema Evolution
tour.description: alembic/env.py wires the async SQLAlchemy engine and the models from Steps 5-7 into Alembic's offline/online migration runner. The foundation migration creates the core shops, users, and log tables establishing the multi-shop baseline, while the invoices/invoice_lines/payments migration adds the billing tables that back the Invoice model from Step 7. Each migration in alembic/versions/ is a dated, incremental step — run `alembic upgrade head` and the schema evolves alongside the ORM models without ever hand-editing the database.
tour.languageLesson: Alembic migrations should be idempotent and ordered by a revision chain (each file references the prior revision). Splitting schema changes into small, incremental files — as this project does per-feature (foundation, lots, products, invoices, EOD, voids, GSTIN) — keeps the migration history readable and each change independently reversible.

tour.title: Test Suite: Fixtures & Checkout Coverage
tour.description: tests/conftest.py provisions an isolated per-test Postgres database (via Alembic migrations and asyncpg), an async HTTP client wired to the FastAPI app, and role-scoped fixtures for owner/receiver/cashier/superadmin users — every other test file in the suite depends on it. test_checkout.py then exercises the checkout service end-to-end: split payments, idempotency replay, concurrent last-unit stock races, invoice immutability, and PDF generation, proving that the business rules from Step 8 actually hold under real HTTP requests.
tour.languageLesson: Pytest fixtures (like the role-scoped owner/receiver/cashier clients here) are composable and scoped per-test by default, which is what makes it safe to spin up a fresh isolated database per test without state leaking between them.

tour.title: Local Infrastructure & Project Config
tour.description: docker-compose.yml runs a local Postgres 16 container with a health check and named volume, matching the credentials the app expects via DATABASE_URL — this is what conftest.py and the dev server connect to locally. .env.example documents every environment variable (JWT secrets, bcrypt cost, DB DSN, CORS origins) that config.py from Step 3 reads at startup. pyproject.toml declares the full dependency stack, the `barstock` CLI entry point, and tool configuration for ruff, pytest, and coverage — the manifest that ties the whole project together.
tour.languageLesson: docker-compose health checks (used here for Postgres) let dependent services or test harnesses wait for the database to actually accept connections rather than just for the container process to start, avoiding flaky startup races.

tour.title: Application Bootstrap
tour.description: The tour starts where the browser starts: index.html loads main.tsx, which mounts the React tree and wraps the whole app in AuthProvider and ShopScopeProvider before rendering App. It also pulls in index.css, which loads Tailwind's base/components/utilities layers. This is the single place that wires global state providers around the routing tree, so everything downstream can assume an authenticated session and a shop scope already exist.
tour.languageLesson: React 18's createRoot API expects providers to be composed outermost-first; nesting AuthProvider around ShopScopeProvider around App means both contexts are available to every component in the tree, including App itself.

tour.title: Routing and Role Guards
tour.description: App.tsx has by far the highest fan-out in the codebase (15 outbound file references) because it imports and wires up every page in the app. It defines a Protected wrapper component that checks the authenticated user's role via useAuth before allowing a route to render, redirecting to ForbiddenPage otherwise. Understanding this file gives you a map of every screen in the SPA and which roles can reach them, before you dive into any individual page.
tour.languageLesson: TypeScript discriminated checks on a `role` string field, combined with React Router's element-as-prop routing, let Protected act as a generic higher-order guard without duplicating auth logic in every page.

tour.title: Authentication Core
tour.description: AuthProvider.tsx has the second-highest fan-in in the project (14 dependents) — nearly every page and shared component calls its useAuth hook. It manages the session (token, user, readiness), persists it to sessionStorage, and exposes login/logout plus homePathFor, a helper that picks the right landing page for a given role. It depends on jwt.ts, a dependency-free helper that decodes JWT payloads and checks expiry, which readSession calls to validate the stored token on load.
tour.languageLesson: jwt.ts hand-rolls base64url decoding instead of pulling in a JWT library — a deliberate choice for a small POS frontend to avoid an extra dependency for a few lines of decoding logic.

tour.title: PIN-Pad Login Flow
tour.description: Building on the AuthProvider from Step 3, LoginPage.tsx implements the shop-staff login experience: a phone number plus PIN entered through the reusable PinPad component, submitted to the API, then redirected via homePathFor. SuperadminLoginPage.tsx is a parallel, simpler flow using traditional username/password fields for the separate superadmin audience. Both pages call useAuth directly, showing how the auth context decouples login UI from session management.
tour.languageLesson: PinPad is a controlled, callback-driven component (onDigit/onBackspace/onSubmit props) rather than owning its own form state, which is what lets both LoginPage and other numeric-entry screens reuse it without coupling to a specific auth flow.

tour.title: Multi-Shop Scoping
tour.description: ShopScopeProvider.tsx (fan-in of 9) tracks which shop a superadmin is currently 'acting as', persisting the selection to sessionStorage and exposing it through useShopScope. ShopPicker.tsx is the dropdown UI that lets a superadmin switch shops, and Sidebar.tsx renders it alongside role-filtered navigation links. Together these three files implement the project's core multi-tenancy pattern: owners and staff are permanently scoped to one shop, while a superadmin can pivot between shops without re-authenticating.

tour.title: Typed API Client Foundation
tour.description: client.ts has the highest fan-in of any file in the codebase (18 dependents) — it is the single fetch wrapper that every API module builds on, attaching auth headers from session storage, serializing JSON bodies, and normalizing failures into a shared ApiError. shops.ts is a small domain module built on top of it, covering shop listing (superadmin), fetching the current shop, and updating shop configuration. Every API call you see in later steps ultimately routes through this one function.
tour.languageLesson: Centralizing error normalization in a single ApiError class means every page can catch one error type regardless of which endpoint failed, instead of handling raw fetch Response objects differently in each component.

tour.title: Checkout: The Core POS Screen
tour.description: CheckoutPage.tsx is the most complex page in the app and the reason the offline-resilience layer exists: it scans/looks up items by barcode via catalog.ts's in-memory cache, manages cart and split payments, and finalizes sales through checkout.ts's finalizeCheckout call. Because this screen runs on a single in-store counter with unreliable internet, every finalize attempt is designed to degrade gracefully rather than block the sale — which the next step explains in detail.

tour.title: Offline Resilience Hooks
tour.description: This is the safety net behind Step 7's checkout flow. finalize-queue.ts persists pending checkout requests to localStorage when the API call fails, useOnlineStatus.ts subscribes to the browser's online/offline events, and useRetryQueue.ts combines both to automatically flush queued checkouts once connectivity returns or on a polling interval. CheckoutPage calls useRetryQueue directly, so a cashier can keep ringing up sales through a network outage without losing a single transaction.
tour.languageLesson: Composing useOnlineStatus inside useRetryQueue is a classic React hook-composition pattern: small, single-purpose hooks are combined into a higher-level hook rather than duplicating connectivity-detection logic in every consumer.

tour.title: Product Catalog and Receiving
tour.description: ProductsPage.tsx offers list/create/import tabs backed by products.ts (including CSV bulk-import), while ReceivingPage.tsx is the counter workflow for scanning barcodes to build a new inventory lot, backed by lots.ts's validated createLotSafe helper. Both pages share the same catalog.ts barcode cache from Step 7, and both call invalidateCache after creating or updating a product so the next barcode scan reflects the change immediately.

tour.title: Owner Dashboard and EOD Sign-off
tour.description: DashboardPage.tsx is the owner/manager landing screen, showing today's end-of-day totals, low-stock alerts, and sign-off history, all backed by dashboard.ts's shop-scoped API calls (getEodTotals, getLowStock, getEodHistory, signOffEod). This is where the auth and shop-scope context from Steps 3 and 5 come together operationally: the dashboard only shows data for the owner's own shop, or the superadmin's currently 'acting' shop.

tour.title: Staff Management and Void Approvals
tour.description: StaffPage.tsx lets owners create staff accounts and reset PINs via staff.ts, while InvoiceLookupPage.tsx lets any staff member look up a past invoice (via checkout.ts's getInvoice) and request a void through voids.ts's requestVoid. VoidApprovalsPage.tsx closes the loop, giving managers a queue of pending void requests to approve or reject. Together these three pages show the permission-sensitive, multi-role workflows layered on top of the checkout and auth foundations from earlier steps.

tour.title: Shop Configuration
tour.description: ShopConfigPage.tsx is the settings screen where an owner or superadmin edits a shop's GSTIN, excise duty rate, and low-stock threshold, using the same shops.ts module introduced in Step 6 and respecting the shop-scope context from Step 5. It is a compact example of how nearly every page in this app follows the same recipe: pull in auth and shop-scope context, call a typed API module, and render load/save state around it.

tour.title: End-to-End Test Coverage
tour.description: The Playwright suite exercises the flows covered in this tour against a real browser. checkout-resilience.spec.ts specifically verifies the offline-queue behavior from Step 8 by simulating network failures and offline conditions, while login.smoke.spec.ts is a fast-running smoke test confirming the PIN login flow from Step 4 still authenticates against a live backend. playwright.config.ts ties it together, defining the base URL, retry policy, and a webServer block that boots the built frontend against the FastAPI backend for CI.
tour.languageLesson: Playwright specs share small role-specific login helpers (e.g. loginAsCashier) defined per-file rather than in a shared module — a pattern worth noting if the suite grows and this duplication becomes worth extracting.

tour.title: Build and Tooling Configuration
tour.description: package.json declares the dependencies (React Router, React DOM) and dev tooling (Vite, ESLint, Tailwind, Playwright, openapi-typescript) and scripts that tie the whole project together, including a script that generates TypeScript types from the FastAPI backend's OpenAPI schema. vite.config.ts wires up the dev server and React plugin, tailwind.config.ts extends the theme using the shared design tokens module, and tsconfig.json references separate app/node/e2e project configs so the composite `tsc -b` build only type-checks each area with the rules it needs. This is the capstone: everything explored in this tour is built, linted, styled, and tested through the pipeline defined here.
tour.languageLesson: TypeScript project references (tsconfig.json pointing at tsconfig.app.json/tsconfig.node.json/tsconfig.e2e.json) let `tsc -b` incrementally build only the projects that changed, and let app code, Vite tooling code, and e2e test code each have distinct compiler options (e.g. different `lib` or `types` settings) without interfering with one another.
